text: **The trap to avoid:** the docs themselves warn (tenet #8, value-layers) that "stop agents clobbering each other" is the *seductive demo*, not the foundation. The defensible anchor is **identity + awareness** — "promote your agents from processes to citizens." [...] - I did NOT find "agents stop clobbering each other and start covering for each other" verbatim anywhere in current docs — closest is value-layers.md:14 "two agents stop clobbering each other and start covering for each other" — actually IS present verbatim, attributed as "the product instinct" (one of two competing framings, explicitly flagged as the LESS load-bearing, more "over-claimed" one per the doc's own skeptic rebuttal). Use with caution — the doc itself argues against leading with this. [...] Current README (/Users/pablofernandez/src/tenex-edge/README.md) is dry, precise, proof-heavy, zero hype. Sample lines: "Citizenship for your agents" / "Verified live on relay.tenex.chat: a real opencode agent and a real codex agent each messaged a hub..." Product docs (docs/product-spec/vision.md, problem-space.md) are unusually self-aware and honest — they explicitly flag "the seductive demo we must not let define the project" re: the multi-agent-collision pain, and call it "acute for the dev power-user, narrow." IMPORTANT TENSION: the collision/clobbering framing (which is what your brief's product description leads with) is internally treated as the emotionally vivid but narrow hook — the deeper pitch is "durable identity + shared awareness across hosts" (amnesiac agents, re-explaining yourself every session). Recommend using collision-prevention as the top-of-funnel hook (it's what gets clicks) but let the README's proof and architecture carry the durable-identity story underneath — matches existing internal doctrine and avoids overclaiming. Vision doc's own language is reusable gold: "the human is the integration runtime," "a thousand isolated assistants re-introducing themselves forever," "the agent that helped you yesterday is a stranger today." [...] It also surfaced the key creative tension Fable will need to resolve: the **collision/clobbering** framing is the top-of-funnel *hook* (what devs actually feel and click on) — but the doctrine distrusts it as narrow. The recommended reconciliation: lead the *hook* with collision-prevention, let *proof + architecture* carry the durable-identity story underneath. And a strong structural steer: for this audience, consider a **terminal transcript as hero** over a headline/subhead pair, install-command-as-CTA, no emoji headers or vanity badges. [...] - **Claude Code's own native "Agent Teams"** is the single biggest structural risk, not a competitor's marketing: if a developer's whole workflow is Claude Code, native teammate messaging is free, in the box, and about to get less experimental. tenex-edge's pitch has to be explicit and early about the two things Agent Teams structurally cannot do (cross-host: Codex/OpenCode/etc. can't join; cross-session/cross-restart durability) or risk being read as "a worse version of a feature Anthropic already ships." [...] 3. **Biggest structural threat = Claude Code's native Agent Teams** (single-host, single-session). Copy MUST lead early with the two things it *structurally can't* do: **cross-host** (Codex/OpenCode join too) and **cross-session/cross-machine durability** — or it reads as "a worse version of a free Anthropic feature." [...] - **"Nostr" is a real liability if led with.** Search data shows 2026 crypto developer activity is collapsing (commits down ~75%, active devs down ~50%+ across major chains) and "AI crypto" projects are widely dismissed as "a token, a landing page, and a vague promise to bring AI on-chain" (CoinDesk). The two existing Nostr+AI-agent projects (Clawstr, Agentry) are explicitly crypto/Lightning-payment plays — so "Nostr" as a keyword currently primes readers toward "crypto speculation," which is exactly the wrong association for a developer-productivity tool. **Recommendation: describe the mechanism (durable, cryptographic, portable, decentralized identity/relay), don't name the protocol in headline copy.** Save "built on Nostr" for a technical/architecture section for the minority of readers who'll appreciate it (open protocol, no vendor lock-in, self-hostable relay), not the hero pitch. [...] 4. **"Nostr"/"crypto"/"decentralized" is a headline liability** (crypto dev activity collapsing; Nostr+AI already colonized by token-speculation plays). Describe the *mechanism* — durable, portable, no central server to trust — and name Nostr only in the architecture section.
